# Local Development Guide

## Standalone Operator Mode

### Standard Way from Generated Files

The grafana-app-sdk generates a standard way of running a kubernetes cluster locally with grafana, datasources, and app setup using k3d and tilt.

#### Prerequisites

* [k3d](https://k3d.io/stable/)
* [tilt](https://tilt.dev/)

#### Create Cluster

The generated Makefile has the target `make local/up` to create the cluster and apply everything. This runs:
* `grafana-app-sdk project local generate` - generates a k3d config (`./local/generated/k3d-config.json`) to mount the host volume `./local/mounted-files` and a YAML bundle in `./local/generated/dev-bundle.yaml`.
* `./local/scripts/cluster.sh create "local/generated/k3d-config.json"` to create the cluster
* `tilt up` in the `local` directory to stand up all resources.

`local/Tiltfile` is generated by `project init` and will scrape all YAML in `local/generated`, and then all YAML in `local/additional` (which overwrites anything declared in `local/generated`).

#### Push Operator Image

The operator image must be built with `make build` and then pushed to the local cluster with `make local/push_operator`

#### Deploy Plugin

The plugin for the app can be pushed to the local grafana deployment with `make local/deploy_plugin`. The plugin must have been built already (`make build`). By default, the grafana deployment loads the plugin from `local/mounted-files/plugin/dist`.

#### Configuration

The local setup can be configured with the file `local/config.yaml` (generated by `project init`).

See `local-config.md` for a full reference of all available configuration options.

#### Testing with kubectl

Once CRDs are applied and the operator is running, create test resources directly:

```bash
# Create a resource
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: my-app.grafana.app/v1alpha1
kind: MyKind
metadata:
  name: test-resource
  namespace: default
spec:
  title: "Test"
  description: "A test resource"
EOF

# Watch the resource status update as the reconciler runs
kubectl get mykind test-resource -w

# See full status
kubectl describe mykind test-resource

# Delete it
kubectl delete mykind test-resource
```

#### Debug Logging

By default, the generated operator code uses debug-level logging when it instantiates the global default logger:
```go
// main.go
func main() {
  // Configure the default logger to use slog
	logging.DefaultLogger = logging.NewSLogLogger(slog.NewJSONHandler(os.Stdout, &slog.HandlerOptions{
		Level: slog.LevelDebug,
	}))
  // ... rest of code
}
```
An app author may change this to read from a config file or CLI, in order to find our, either ask the user or read `cmd/operator/main.go` to see how the logging is configured.

### Alternative Local Dev Methods

#### Prerequisites

A local Kubernetes cluster is required. The easiest options:

```bash
# Option A: kind (Kubernetes in Docker)
brew install kind
kind create cluster --name dev

# Option B: minikube
brew install minikube
minikube start

# Confirm kubectl is pointing at the local cluster
kubectl config current-context
kubectl get nodes
```

#### Apply CRDs

Before running the operator, apply the generated CRDs to the cluster:

```bash
# Apply all generated CRD files
kubectl apply -f definitions/
```

> `kubectl apply -f definitions/` will also attempt to apply the `<app>-manifest.(json|yaml)` file, which is not a valid Kubernetes resource and will produce an error. This error can be safely ignored — the CRD files will still be applied successfully.

Run this again after every `grafana-app-sdk generate` if schema fields changed.

#### Run the Operator Locally

Build and run the operator binary against the local cluster:

```bash
# Run directly with go run
go run ./cmd/operator/... --kubeconfig="$HOME/.kube/config"

# Or build first then run
make build
./bin/operator --kubeconfig="$HOME/.kube/config"
```

The operator uses the standard kubeconfig by default — it will connect to whatever cluster `kubectl` points to.

---

## grafana/apps Mode

For grafana/apps, local development means running the full Grafana binary with your app registered.

### Build and Run Grafana

From the Grafana repo root:

```bash
# Build the backend (includes your app)
make build-go

# Run Grafana with your app registered
./bin/grafana server

# Or use the dev mode that watches for changes
make run
```

Your app is automatically included in the binary because it's registered in `pkg/registry/apps/apps.go`.

### Testing with Grafana UI

1. Navigate to `http://localhost:3000`
2. Use the Grafana API or UI to create resources of your kind
3. Watch server logs for reconciler/admission output

### API Access

Your kind is accessible via the Grafana API at:

```
GET/POST /apis/<group>/<version>/namespaces/<namespace>/<plural>
```

For example:
```bash
# List resources
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  http://localhost:3000/apis/my-app.grafana.app/v1alpha1/namespaces/default/mykinds

# Create a resource
curl -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"apiVersion":"my-app.grafana.app/v1alpha1","kind":"MyKind","metadata":{"name":"test"},"spec":{"title":"Test"}}' \
  http://localhost:3000/apis/my-app.grafana.app/v1alpha1/namespaces/default/mykinds
```

---

## Common Development Patterns

### Watching Reconciler Behavior

Run the operator with debug logging and watch what happens when resources change:

```bash
# Terminal 1: Run operator
go run ./cmd/operator/...

# Terminal 2: Create a resource and watch it
kubectl apply -f my-resource.yaml && kubectl get mykind -w
```

### Forcing a Re-reconcile

The operator reconciles on changes. To force a re-reconcile without editing the resource:

```bash
# Touch the resource with a no-op annotation change
kubectl annotate mykind my-resource grafana.com/force-reconcile="$(date)" --overwrite
```

### Checking Admission Webhooks

For standalone mode, the operator runs its own webhook server. Test admission by creating/updating resources and checking for validation errors:

```bash
# Invalid resource (should be rejected)
kubectl apply -f invalid-resource.yaml
# Output should include the admission error message

# Check webhook logs in the operator output
```

### Resetting State

To start fresh during development:

```bash
# Delete all resources of a kind
kubectl delete mykind --all -n default

# Delete and re-apply CRDs (loses all data)
kubectl delete -f definitions/
kubectl apply -f definitions/
```

> Both commands will produce an error for the `<app>-manifest.(json|yaml)` file, which is not a valid Kubernetes resource. These errors can be safely ignored.
